docs.title: Aetherion
docs.text: A homebrew Pathfinder 2e setting. This site collects everything the players know — the campaign primer, the public world lore, and the player's guide.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Player's Guide — the campaign-specific guide for character creation, themes, tone, and table expectations. Read this first.
  • The World You Know — what every Skyborn knows about the world. The in-world primer.
  • Backgrounds — campaign-specific PF2e backgrounds.

docs.title: Browse the world
docs.text: Use the navigation tree on the left, or the search bar above.

docs.title: About this site
docs.text: This is a curated, player-facing slice of the full Aetherion design notes. GM-only material is held back at the source; the world reveals itself in play.

docs.title: The Antiquarian
docs.text: An old shopkeeper in Aurion's mid-quarter. Collector and dealer in old objects, scholar of vanished religions, weirdly well-informed about ruins, relics, and obscure history. He pays well for relic-recovery work and employs intermediaries to handle the field jobs. Business associates call him the Antiquarian; those who know him better call him Yverin.

docs.title: The shop
docs.text: A narrow storefront on Brasswick Alley — three doors down from a stationer, two up from a bakery whose rolls are stale by midmorning. The brass plate above the door reads simply ANTIQUITIES in archaic script. Inside: leather, dry paper, the lingering scent of bergamot. Hundreds of objects shelved with no obvious system. A counter at the back. A kettle behind the counter, perpetually warm. A door behind the kettle that only certain people are ever invited through.

docs.title: Meeting him
docs.text:
• Stooped, perpetually a little cold, weathered hands with ink-stained fingertips. Spectacles with one lens slightly cracked. A heavy wool coat indoors, year-round.
  • Wry, patient, a little sad. Listens more than he speaks.
  • Pours tea unprompted when a difficult conversation is about to begin.
  • Pays well and pays on time.
  • Knows more than he ought to about places and things he has no obvious connection to.
  • Won't say what he was before he was the Antiquarian.
  • His name — Yverin — doesn't quite fit any culture's naming convention. Most people who notice assume it's an old family form or a scholarly affectation.

docs.title: What people say
docs.text: In the mid-quarter, the Antiquarian is well-liked in the way an eccentric neighbor is well-liked: he's been there a long time, he hasn't troubled anyone, he sometimes pays a too-generous price for an old curio someone was about to throw out. Nobody asks too many questions, partly because he doesn't invite them and partly because the answers he gives are pleasant enough not to need following.

docs.title: Backgrounds (Campaign-Specific)
docs.text:
Pathfinder 2e's standard backgrounds all work in Aetherion. The list below adds campaign-specific options — picks that give your character a deeper foothold in the world, a tie to a faction, or a story-hook the GM already knows how to use.

Each grants the standard PF2e background package: a free ability boost plus a choice between two listed boosts, training in one skill, training in one lore skill, and a granted skill feat.

A few backgrounds are tagged "Talk to the GM" — these have heretical or politically sensitive ties in the setting. The tag isn't a "no"; it's a "drop me a line first so the campaign opening can handle your situation gracefully." Approvals are usually quick.

Don't see your concept? Reach out to the GM. If you have a character idea that isn't on the list — a different profession, a different upbringing, a hook this slate doesn't capture — we can build a custom background for it together. Custom backgrounds get the same standard PF2e package (boost choice + free boost, trained skill, trained lore, granted skill feat); we just pick the four pieces that fit your concept. Bring me a sentence or two about who your character was before the campaign and we'll work backwards from there.

Mechanical references (skills, feats) below link to the Archives of Nethys for quick reference. Lore skills are campaign-specific to Aetherion and don't have AoN entries.

docs.title: Shop Apprentice
docs.text:
You apprenticed to Yverin at his antique shop in Aurion's mid-quarter for a year or more. You know where things are kept, which customers to be wary of, and which days the deliveries come.

Boost: Int or Cha · Skill: Society · Lore: Aurion · Feat: Streetwise

docs.title: Yverin's Courier
docs.text:
You run the Antiquarian's deliveries across Aurion and occasionally farther. You know the back routes, the discreet drop-houses, and which Skyguard patrols move when.

Boost: Dex or Wis · Skill: Stealth · Lore: Aurion · Feat: Pickpocket

docs.title: The Antiquarian's Youngest
docs.text:
You're the youngest of Yverin's hires, and he's taken a personal interest. He makes you tea. He tells you old stories. You don't know why you, but you don't mind.

Boost: Int or Cha · Skill: Society · Lore: Antiquarian · Feat: Bon Mot

docs.title: The Shop Translator
docs.text:
Yverin's correspondence runs through more languages than is reasonable. You handle the old-tongue paperwork — archaic dwarven, pre-Skyfall scripts, dialects no one speaks aloud anymore.

Boost: Int or Wis · Skill: Society · Lore: Old-Tongue · Feat: Multilingual

docs.title: Inquisition Castaway (Talk to the GM)
docs.text:
You were a sworn Skyward Inquisitor or initiate, and you left. Whether they let you go or you ran, they know your name and your record.

Boost: Str or Wis · Skill: Religion · Lore: Inquisition · Feat: Recognize Spell

docs.title: Crown Assembly Page
docs.text:
You served a noble House of the Crown Assembly — kept their schedules, carried their letters, learned which fork was for which course. You know how Highcrown politics actually works.

Boost: Cha or Int · Skill: Diplomacy · Lore: Heraldry · Feat: Courtly Graces

docs.title: Disgraced Heir
docs.text:
You were once a minor House heir. A feud, a scandal, a wrong cousin — your name was taken. The manners and the connections remain.

Boost: Cha or Int · Skill: Deception · Lore: Genealogy · Feat: Lie to Me

docs.title: Bank of Aurion Clerk
docs.text:
You kept ledgers in one of Aurion's great mercantile houses. The accounts told you whose money came from where; the things rich families try to hide eventually pass through a clerk's desk.

Boost: Int or Wis · Skill: Society · Lore: Mercantile · Feat: Quick Identification

docs.title: Skyguard Veteran
docs.text:
You served honorably in the Skyguard. Whether you mustered out, were invalided, or just walked away — the discipline stuck, and so did the wounds.

Boost: Str or Con · Skill: Athletics · Lore: Warfare · Feat: Hefty Hauler

docs.title: Cloud Knight Squire
docs.text:
You almost made knighthood with the Cloud Knights of Aurion. Almost. You can wear the gear; you can quote the codes; the spurs were never struck.

Boost: Str or Cha · Skill: Diplomacy · Lore: Heraldry · Feat: Group Impression

docs.title: Sylvari Archivist's Aide
docs.text:
You apprenticed at the Archivists of Moonbough — the Great Library beneath the canopy city. You can read in the dim; you can find a misshelved scroll by smell.

Boost: Int or Wis · Skill: Society · Lore: Archive · Feat: Assurance (Society)

docs.title: Aetheric Collegium Initiate
docs.text:
You trained as a sanctioned shard-scholar at the Collegium. You've handled relics under proper authority and passed your initiate's tests. The Collegium watches what you do with the credential.

Boost: Int or Wis · Skill: Arcana · Lore: Shard · Feat: Recognize Spell

docs.title: Lanternbearer's Apprentice (Talk to the GM)
docs.text:
Your teacher was a Lanternbearer — one of the outlawed shard-mystics whose practice the Inquisition does not recognize. They taught you anyway. You hope your name isn't on a list.

Boost: Wis or Cha · Skill: Occultism · Lore: Shard · Feat: Trick Magic Item

docs.title: Failed Apprentice
docs.text:
You washed out of an academy — Crowngear's, the Sylvari conservatories, a noble house's private tutoring program. You know the systems from the inside. The systems don't miss you.

Boost: Int (plus free) · Skill: Crafting or Society · Lore: pick one (Crowngear / Sylvari / Aurion / etc.) · Feat: Quick Identification

docs.title: Khalvar Forge-Soldier
docs.text:
You trained in Khalvar Hold's tradition of warrior-craftsmen — soldiers who keep their own gear, smiths who can hold a line. The forge is a battlefield; the battlefield is a forge.

Boost: Str or Con · Skill: Crafting · Lore: Warfare · Feat: Specialty Crafting

docs.title: Ember Faith Pilgrim (Talk to the GM)
docs.text:
You walked the mourning routes of the Ember Faith — the dwarven splinter sect that keeps vigil for the lost Red Ember. The Court tolerates them as fringe. Tolerance, you've learned, is a thin thing.

Boost: Wis or Con · Skill: Religion · Lore: Ember Faith · Feat: Student of the Canon

docs.title: Black Anvil Initiate (Talk to the GM)
docs.text:
You ran with the Black Anvil — Ankarum's heretic cult that searches the mountain interior for "ancient shard-forges." Whether you came up with anything, you know the deep tunnels.

Boost: Con or Int · Skill: Crafting · Lore: Underground · Feat: Specialty Crafting (Blacksmithing)

docs.title: Keelwright Mason
docs.text:
You apprenticed with the Keelwrights of Ankarum — the engineering guild that quite literally holds Aetherion together. Stone, rune, and load-bearing arches are your trade.

Boost: Str or Int · Skill: Crafting · Lore: Engineering · Feat: Specialty Crafting (Stonemasonry)

docs.title: Crowngear Engineer
docs.text:
You trained at Cogbarrow under the Gearwright's Consortium. Drift control, turbines, the calibrated madness that keeps Aetherion in the air — you can read the schematics and fix the parts.

Boost: Int or Dex · Skill: Crafting · Lore: Crowngear · Feat: Specialty Crafting

docs.title: Mausoleum Attendant
docs.text:
You worked the Last Gate Mausoleum in Cogbarrow — gnomish funerary craft, clockwork ossuaries, the precision of mourning. The dead get the dignity of the very best engineering.

Boost: Wis or Con · Skill: Religion · Lore: Mortuary · Feat: Student of the Canon

docs.title: Caravan Outrider
docs.text:
You rode with the League of Feathered Roads — the halfling caravan-guild whose routes web the continent. Every skybridge worth crossing, you've crossed.

Boost: Dex or Con · Skill: Survival · Lore: Sky Travel · Feat: Forager

docs.title: Hedge Witch's Apprentice
docs.text:
You learned from a Tumblebrook hedge witch — the unofficial folk-magic of the countryside, the cures and the curses and the way to read a sky for rain. Quiet work, mostly tolerated.

Boost: Wis or Cha · Skill: Nature or Occultism · Lore: Herbalism · Feat: Natural Medicine

docs.title: Festival Officiate
docs.text:
You're Mirth's lay clergy — the people who actually organize the festivals while the gods get the credit. Lights, music, food, fire-safety, drunken pilgrims, gentle drunks at dawn. You handle it.

Boost: Cha or Con · Skill: Performance · Lore: Festival · Feat: Fascinating Performance

docs.title: Tether's End Native
docs.text:
You grew up in the descent town — the only place on the continent that touches the world below. You've seen lifts move things no one talks about. You've seen smugglers stop talking when you walked in.

Boost: Dex or Wis · Skill: Survival · Lore: Tether's End · Feat: Streetwise

docs.title: Salvage Hauler
docs.text:
You worked the lift gangs at the Grand Anchor — pulling wreckage, crates, the occasional thing that should not have come up. The Salvage Syndicate took its cut. You took yours quietly.

Boost: Str or Wis · Skill: Athletics · Lore: Salvage · Feat: Hefty Hauler

docs.title: Hospice Volunteer
docs.text:
You worked at one of Mercy's hospices — the largest is Aurelium Hospice in Aurion, but the network runs everywhere. Bedside hours, long ones. You know death and you know what to say to the dying.

Boost: Wis or Cha · Skill: Medicine · Lore: Mercy · Feat: Battle Medicine

docs.title: Cloistered Devotee
docs.text:
You were raised in a temple to one of the Twelve. You knew the daily rites before you knew the alphabet. You have, possibly, lapsed. The training stuck.

Boost: Wis or Cha · Skill: Religion · Lore: pick a specific god · Feat: Student of the Canon

docs.title: Mortis's Witness
docs.text:
You were present at a death of consequence — a soldier in honest cause, a healer mid-rescue, a maker finishing one last piece. Mortis came. You remember. Some people say the look in your eyes hasn't quite settled since.

Boost: Wis or Con · Skill: Religion · Lore: Mortis · Feat: Diehard

docs.title: Skyfall Survivor
docs.text:
You were caught in a Skyfall event as a child and lived. The experience left you... attuned to certain stones. You can feel them before you see them.

Boost: Con or Wis · Skill: Occultism · Lore: Skyfall · Feat: Shard Sense (campaign-specific feat, coming)

docs.title: Streetwise Orphan
docs.text:
You grew up in Aurion's underbelly — the alleys, the rooftops, the kitchens where the back door is always unlocked. You know every alley because the alleys were home.

Boost: Dex or Cha · Skill: Stealth · Lore: Aurion · Feat: Pickpocket

docs.title: Awakened animal
docs.text: These give an awakened animal a place in the world. Talk to the GM about which fits your concept; we'll work out the rules path together.

docs.title: Caged Wonder
docs.text:
You were an awakened animal in an oddities-collector's menagerie — a curiosity, a conversation piece, a thing the rich pointed at over wine. You're out now.

Boost: Con or Cha · Skill: Acrobatics · Lore: Aurion · Feat: Cat Fall

docs.title: Hospice Companion
docs.text:
You were an awakened animal kept by Mercy's hospices for therapeutic work — bedside visits to the dying, gentle presence in long nights. They let you choose your own path when you asked.

Boost: Wis or Con · Skill: Medicine · Lore: Mercy · Feat: Natural Medicine

docs.title: What if I don't see what I want?
docs.text: Talk to the GM. Backgrounds are the easiest thing in the system to bend or build new from scratch — if you have a specific concept that doesn't fit any of the above, we'll figure it out together.

docs.title: Aetherion — Player's Guide
docs.text:
Welcome to Aetherion, a homebrew Pathfinder 2e campaign. You'll be playing levels 1 through 20 across (we expect) a multi-year run, Saturdays 6-10pm. This guide is for you to read before the campaign starts so you can build a character that fits.

For the in-world primer — what every Skyborn knows about the continent, the Pantheon, the Pit, and the nations — see The World You Know. This guide focuses on this campaign specifically: the shape of the story, how you're connected, and what kinds of characters will thrive.

Throughout this guide, mechanical references (classes, feats, skills, etc.) link to the Archives of Nethys for quick reference. Homebrew options link to our own design docs.

docs.title: What this campaign is
docs.text:
A 1-20 epic in the home setting your GM has been building. Some shape notes:

  • Anthology at the start; cohesive at the end. Early sessions are mostly self-contained jobs — recover a thing, escort a person, investigate a body. By the middle of the campaign, threads will start braiding into something larger. By the end, you'll be on the kind of quest that L1 characters would have been laughed at for accepting.
  • Stakes that grow with you. You won't start L1 trying to save the world. You'll be on that kind of quest by L20, but you'll have earned it.
  • Plot you can see. Your GM has a story he wants to tell. He's not going to railroad you, but the tracks are there and visible. Take hooks; follow plot threads. If you want to go somewhere unexpected, say so — the GM will pivot.
  • Mystery as content, not as gameplay. You don't have to solve a puzzle to advance the story. The world reveals itself as you play.

This is not a Kingmaker-style sandbox or a kingdom-building game. There will be some sandbox stretches, but the overall shape is "growing into a story" rather than "building a place."

docs.title: What this campaign is about
docs.text:
Themes you'll encounter, in case they help you pick a character:

  • Working for someone whose motives aren't entirely clear, and choosing to trust them anyway
  • The gap between official histories and what the records actually say
  • Communities of outsiders carving out lives in a world that wasn't built for them
  • The cost of mercy and the cost of cruelty
  • Becoming powerful enough to matter to gods

If you're drawn to characters who chafe against authority, who ask questions, or who quietly take care of people the world has forgotten — there's room for you here. If you'd rather play a true believer who slowly has to reckon with what they were taught — there's room for you too.

docs.title: Where the campaign starts
docs.text:
You all live or work in Aurion, capital of Highcrown — the political and religious center of Aetherion. The Skyward Inquisition headquarters here; so does the Majestal Court. It's a city of marble, smoke, scripture, and the loud whisper of orthodoxy.

You've each ended up taking work from a quirky shopkeeper named Yverin, who calls himself the Antiquarian. He runs a mid-district shop full of old relics, older books, and what most people would call useless dust. He pays well for small jobs — recoveries, deliveries, the occasional discreet errand. He's widely considered eccentric but harmless. He's old. He's wry. He talks too much about ancient history and not enough about himself.

You don't ask too many questions. The pay is good.

docs.title: Why you're together
docs.text:
The group's agreed framing: you work for the same private patron — Yverin. Beyond that, you decide your relationships. Were you hired together? Hired separately and met on the job? Old friends who answered the same ad? Strangers who learned to be useful to each other? Up to you.

Some questions to think about for your character:

  • How did Yverin find you, or how did you find him?
  • What did he offer that you needed?
  • Why do you keep coming back?

docs.title: Basics
docs.text:
• Starting level: 1
  • Ancestry: any PF2e ancestry your GM approves (see notes below)
  • Class: any PF2e class (see notes below)
  • Background: standard PF2e backgrounds work; campaign-specific backgrounds are in development (see below)
  • Ability scores: standard PF2e creation
  • Hit points & equipment: standard

docs.title: Ancestries — what's common
docs.text:
The five Skyborn ancestries dominate Aetherion society. Each has a "home" nation and a cultural reputation, though plenty of people live outside their home:

  • Human (AoN) — Highcrown majority; politically and religiously dominant; the "default" ancestry of the continent's power structures.
  • Elf (AoN) — Sylvaris majority; scholarly, long-lived, somewhat insular; lots of contact with the Archivists of Moonbough.
  • Dwarf (AoN) — Ankarum majority; fortress culture, formal, structural engineering; the Keelwrights literally hold the continent together.
  • Halfling (AoN) — Tumblebrook majority; agricultural, communal, gentle; feeds the rest of Aetherion.
  • Gnome (AoN) — Glymswick majority; inventive, irreverent, mechanical; the Gearwright Consortium runs Crowngear and the drift control that keeps Aetherion mobile.

docs.title: Ancestries — versatile heritages
docs.text: Half-elves, half-orcs, and tieflings all exist in Aetherion. They're more common than polite society admits. The Skyward Inquisition gets twitchy about visible "hybrids" but rarely acts on it openly in cosmopolitan Aurion. If you want to play a character with an unusual heritage — a striking visual feature, a partial lineage, something the world doesn't have a name for — talk to the GM; we'll find a way to make it work.

docs.title: Ancestries — uncommon and rare
docs.text:
• Awakened Animal: Exists, but extremely rare in Aetherion. The Inquisition treats awakened animals with suspicion. If you want to play one, talk to the GM — there's a campaign-specific path for this concept that gives your character an interesting place in the world.
  • Other Lost Omens ancestries: Generally available with GM approval. Some will fit Aetherion cleanly; some will need a conversation.

docs.title: Classes — what fits where
docs.text:
Aetherion is friendly to almost every PF2e class. Some notes that might shape your concept:

Divine and faith-based - Cleric / Champion of the Twelve — pillars of Skyborn society. Public, respected, and watched by the Inquisition. Lots of mechanical and narrative support. - Cleric / Champion of an unsanctioned power — technically possible, deeply unwise. Don't pick this without a long conversation. - Oracle — exists but draws attention. Strange visions are often diagnosed as illness or possession by orthodox priests.

Arcane - Wizard / Magus — the Sylvari academies are the seat of formal arcane study, but practitioners exist across all five nations. - Witch — exists; eccentric patrons raise eyebrows. - Sorcerer — common; bloodline backgrounds work as normal.

Primal and occult - Druid — exists, but Skyborn druidic traditions favor sky and storm and wind over deep-forest nature. - Bard — beloved everywhere; the festival circuits in Tumblebrook and the court masques in Aurion both want them. - Psychic — uncommon, mystical, draws attention from the Inquisition.

Martial - Fighter / Barbarian / Ranger — common in all nations. - Rogue / Investigator — Aurion has more shadow than light. Plenty of room. - Monk — uncommon, considered exotic; some Sylvari traditions support it. - Swashbuckler / Gunslinger — both fit; gunslingers especially work for Glymswick characters or Cogbarrow-trained ones.

Newer classes - Kineticist — rare but accepted. An esoteric tradition. Aetherion mythologizes air, water, metal, and wood more than earth and fire, but all elements exist. - Alchemist — common in Glymswick; the Crowngear Consortium loves an alchemist. - Magus / Thaumaturge / Inventor / Summoner — all available; expect some side-eye from the orthodoxy depending on flavor. - Commander — Khalvar Hold's strategists; Ankarum produces a lot of these. - Guardian — common across the five nations; "stand in front, take the hit" is a respected role.

If a class isn't on this list, it's still probably available — ask.

docs.title: Backgrounds
docs.text:
All standard PF2e backgrounds work. We've also written a slate of campaign-specific backgrounds — see the full list with mechanical details at Backgrounds (Campaign-Specific).

Picks include shop work for Yverin, Highcrown institutions (Inquisition, Crown Assembly, Skyguard), Sylvari scholarship, Ankarum dwarven craft, Glymswick engineering, Tumblebrook pastoral, Tether's End liminal, religious vocations, outsider/wanderer, and awakened-animal-friendly options. Some are tagged "Talk to the GM" — they have heretical or politically sensitive ties and just need a quick chat before character creation.

If none fit your concept, talk to the GM — backgrounds are easy to bend or build from scratch.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Skills that pay off across the campaign:

  • Society — political, factional, and historical knowledge. Will be useful constantly.
  • Religion — public theology of the Twelve, the calendar, the iconography.
  • Arcana / Occultism — shards and weird relics show up a lot.
  • Stealth / Thievery / Deception — Yverin's work is not always above-board.
  • Diplomacy — for talking to fences, nobles, scholars, and the occasional Inquisitor.

Useful but not central:

  • Athletics — always pays off in combat-heavy sessions.
  • Survival — most of the campaign starts urban but the wilderness pays off later.
  • Nature — strong for druids/rangers; less central than Religion/Society for most.
  • Performance — surprisingly useful for an Aurion-set early game.

docs.title: Lore skills worth picking up
docs.text:
If your background gives you a lore slot or you want to grab one with a skill increase:

  • Aurion Lore — the home city; streets, factions, neighborhoods.
  • Inquisition Lore — how they operate, who their senior figures are.
  • Skyfall Lore — the falling-relic phenomenon and its folklore.
  • Crowngear Lore — Glymswick technology, drift control, mechanical artifice.
  • Sky Travel Lore — bridges, ropeways, caravans, airships.

docs.title: Useful feats to consider
docs.text:
This is suggestion, not prescription. Feats that tend to pay off in Aetherion's early sessions:

  • Streetwise / Connections / Group Impression — for navigating Aurion's social fabric.
  • Pickpocket / Subtle Theft — Yverin's errands often shade gray.
  • Recognize Spell / Trick Magic Item — shards and relics are everywhere.
  • Quick Identification / Assurance (Religion or Society) — knowing what's in front of you fast.
  • Diehard / Toughness — Aurion has more violence than its facade suggests.

docs.title: Common, uncommon, and rare options
docs.text:
Pathfinder 2e flags some feats, spells, items, ancestries, and class options as uncommon or rare — meaning you can't just pick them up the same way as common options. In Aetherion, treat that flag as an in-world question: where did your character learn this thing, and is the world ready to admit they know it?

  • Common options are anyone's. Pick freely.
  • Uncommon options need GM approval plus a one-sentence in-fiction reason — a teacher, a relic you found, a tradition you were raised in, a lineage that explains the trait. Most uncommon picks will be cleared cheerfully; the conversation is mostly to weave them into your backstory.
  • Rare options are story-locked. Don't expect to take one at character creation. Expect to earn the opportunity in play. If you have a strong concept that lives at the rare tier, talk to the GM early.

The same logic applies to gear, alchemical formulas, weapon traits, and most class options that aren't flagged common. The answer is usually yes — but the question matters.

docs.title: Shards (campaign-specific mechanic)
docs.text:
You're going to find shards. A lot of them, eventually. A few things to know:

  • Shards are bits of crystallized... something. Old, valuable, weird. They sometimes whisper. They sometimes show wielders flashes of memory that aren't their own.
  • Mechanically: PF2e relics. If you've used relic rules before, that's the framework.
  • No corruption track. Bonding with a shard won't make your character evil or possessed. The pressure is purely narrative.
  • You'll likely bond with one over the campaign. Voluntary. Reversible until very late.
  • More detail at first session. For now: don't avoid shards out of habit, and don't grab them as expected loot either. They have weight.

docs.title: Table expectations
docs.text:
What we ask of you:

  • One character for the campaign. If your character dies, we build a new one together. (Not the focus of this campaign, though — character death will be rare in the published-AP sense.)
  • Show up if you can. We meet weekly. Missing a session is fine; the story is built to tolerate it, and you won't lose your seat or critical story moments.
  • Engage with the world. You don't have to be a master roleplayer. You don't have to do voices. But talk to named NPCs, follow plot hooks you can see, and let the story land.
  • Tell us what you want. If a thread isn't working for you, say so. If you want more focus on something — a class feature, a relationship, a backstory thread — ask. The GM is happy to pivot.
  • Bring a backstory. Even one paragraph is enough. Give the GM something to grab. The earlier you can share it, the more of it can show up in play.

docs.title: Pacing
docs.text:
• Saturday 6-10pm, weekly. Same slot as Kingmaker.
  • This campaign starts after Kingmaker concludes. No firm date yet; track the Nyrissa arc.
  • Roughly 1 level per 4-6 sessions, varying by adventure shape.
  • Multi-year run. Plan accordingly. Build a character you'll still want to play in two years.

docs.title: Backstory prompts
docs.text:
Use as much or as little as helps. Even just a few sentences for each is great.

  1. How did Yverin find you, or how did you find him?
  2. What does he pay you for? What kind of work do you do for him?
  3. What's your relationship to Aurion? Native? Recent arrival? Returning after years away?
  4. What's your relationship to the Twelve? Devout? Lapsed? Cynical? Fearful? Curious?
  5. What's your relationship to the Inquisition? Awe? Distrust? Personal grudge? Indifference?
  6. What do you want? Money? Knowledge? Family? Vengeance? To be left alone? To matter to someone?
  7. What's something other characters would notice about you on first meeting? A scar, a tic, a turn of phrase, an unusual possession.

You don't need to answer all of these. Pick what helps. The GM will work with whatever you give him.

docs.title: Open questions / what's still in flux
docs.text:
This guide is a working draft — it'll evolve as we approach session one. Things still being firmed up:

  • The home nation. Aurion / Highcrown is the working assumption (it's where Yverin's shop is). Talk to the GM if you wanted to build a character from elsewhere with a strong reason to be in Aurion.
  • Final mechanical drafts of the campaign-specific backgrounds. Coming before session 1.
  • Awakened-animal path. Talk to the GM if this concept calls to you.
  • Any rule we should house-rule. If there's a PF2e rule you've found wonky, raise it now.

docs.title: Asking questions
docs.text:
Ask the GM anytime. There are no dumb questions — especially during character creation. The sooner we hear what you're building, the more we can integrate your character into the campaign's opening.

Welcome to Aetherion. The shop is open.

docs.title: The World You Know
docs.text: What every Skyborn knows about the world they live in, without having to roll for it. The story you were taught growing up — by your parents, by your priests, by the festival sermons and the children's rhymes.

docs.title: The world above
docs.text:
You live on Aetherion, the great floating continent suspended in the sky. It has forests, mountains, rivers, farms, cities, and weather, much like any land — but it is held above the world by divine grace and the engineering of the dwarves and gnomes. Most of you will never visit any land that is not Aetherion.

There are five peoples — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Gnome — each with its own nation and place in the harmony of things.

docs.title: The Twelve
docs.text:
Twelve gods watch over Aetherion: Maelstrom, Memory, Mercy, Malice, Majesty, Madness, Mirth, Melancholy, Mortis, Motive, Muse, Might. Together they are the Twelve or the Pantheon.

Some say they are real beings who walk among us. Some say they are aspects of one greater divinity. Some say they are conceptual principles. The Court teaches all three views without contradiction — choose your own, or hold them all.

Majesty is most often seen and is recognized as the publicly-leading god. The others appear more rarely, in places appropriate to their aspect.

docs.title: The Vs — the Fallen Powers
docs.text: The Vs were gods who once tried to drag Aetherion down. The Twelve defeated them. They are now confined to the Underworld. To invoke them is heresy.

docs.title: The Pit below
docs.text:
Below Aetherion lies the Pit — a hellscape of corruption, poisoned air, twisted creatures, and demonic powers. Its inhabitants ("Groundborn") are tainted and dangerous. Descent is forbidden. The Anchor exists only to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage.

The children's rhyme — The Pit Below — is the version most of you learned as kids.

docs.title: Skyfall
docs.text:
The Twelve sometimes scatter fragments of divine light across the heavens — Skyshards — which fall to Aetherion as tests of worthiness. The pure may wield them; the unworthy are destroyed. Today, Skyfalls are rare.

Cultural names differ: - Dwarves call them World-Bones. - Elves call them Starfruit or Moon-amber. - Humans call them Relics.

docs.title: The Moon and Her Sister
docs.text: The moon was once whole, until her jealous sister rose from the deep night and struck her. The clash scattered the sister's bones into the planetary ring above us. The scar on the moon is her wound. Priests call the scar the Eye of Judgment and the ring the Halo of Aetherion.

docs.title: Travel
docs.text: Most of you have never been in a flying vehicle. Skybridges, ropeways, caravans, and river boats run by the halfling League of Feathered Roads move people and goods across Aetherion. The military fly airships, and a few rich nobles or gnomish industrialists have personal craft, but ordinary people walk and ride.

docs.title: The nations, briefly
docs.text:
• Highcrown — Aurion (Human): the political and religious center. The Majestal Court interprets the will of the Twelve from here.
  • Sylvaris — Moonbough (Elf): luminous trees, suspended gardens, observatories. The largest surviving library in the world is here.
  • Ankarum — Khalvar Hold (Dwarf): the Keelwrights keep Aetherion structurally whole. Runic engineering, mining, mountains.
  • Glymswick — Cogbarrow (Gnome): invention, drift control. The Gearwright's Consortium keeps Aetherion moving.
  • Tumblebrook — Greenmere (Halfling): the breadbasket. Farms, pastures, festivals, and the trade roads.
  • Tether's End: the ramshackle village around the Grand Anchor. Smugglers, outcasts, exiles. Most respectable people stay away.

docs.title: The Public Story
docs.text: This is the cosmology as taught on Aetherion. Most Skyborn believe it sincerely.

docs.title: The Twelve
docs.text:
Twelve gods watch over Aetherion from the heavens. Their names are aspects of the human condition: Maelstrom, Memory, Mercy, Malice, Majesty, Madness, Mirth, Melancholy, Mortis, Motive, Muse, Might. They are worshipped collectively as the Twelve or the Pantheon; individual aspect veneration is supplementary.

Three sanctioned views of who/what they are coexist among the faithful — active beings, aspects of a single divinity, or conceptual principles.

docs.title: The Fallen Vs
docs.text: The Vs are the Fallen Powers — gods who once sought to drag Aetherion down into ruin. Defeated by the Twelve, they are now confined to the corruption of the Underworld. To invoke them is heresy. See world/religion/inquisition-policy.md.

docs.title: Skyfall
docs.text:
The Twelve sometimes scatter fragments of divine light across the heavens — Skyshards — that fall to Aetherion as tests of worthiness. The pure may wield them; the unworthy are destroyed. The corrupt below would defile them; the worthy above are entrusted with stewardship.

Skyfalls were frequent centuries ago. Today they are rare.

For the cultural elaborations around shards — the cultural names, the rumored properties — see world/propaganda-and-folklore/shard-folklore.md.

docs.title: The Underworld
docs.text:
Below Aetherion lies the Pit — a hellscape of corruption, poisoned air, twisted creatures, and demonic powers. Its inhabitants ("Groundborn") are tainted: skin marked, eyes wrong, bodies warped. Descent is heresy. Trade with Groundborn is treason. The Anchor exists to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage.

The full Majestal Court sermon on this subject is in world/propaganda-and-folklore/inquisition-warnings.md.

docs.title: The Moon and Her Sister
docs.text:
Folk version: there was once a single shining moon. Her jealous sister rose from the deep night and hurled herself upon her. The clash shattered the sky, scattering the sister's bones into a crown of stars around the world (the ring). The scar on the moon is her wound, weeping light into the dark.

Priests' version: the scar is the Eye of Judgment — watching mortals. The ring is the Halo of Aetherion — proof the gods defend the world against chaos. Some sects claim each fragment is a shard of angelic essence.

Both versions in narrative form: world/propaganda-and-folklore/moon-mythology.md.

docs.title: The Races
docs.text: The five Aetherion ancestries are the chosen peoples, raised to civilization under the protection of the Twelve. The "monstrous" races of the Pit are corruption made flesh.

docs.title: Black Anvil
docs.text:
A small, persistent dwarven heretic cult that believes Aetherion's mountain interior hides ancient caverns of original shard-forges — predating any recorded history, predating even the Keelwrights' rune-craft tradition. They search those caverns, in quiet defiance of Keelwright licensure and occasional Inquisition attention.

The mainstream of Ankarum dwarven society treats them as harmless cranks. The Keelwrights officially condemn them. The Order of the Pure Flame considers them low-priority — the Black Anvil has never produced an actual shard to justify higher attention. The cult has been searching for over a thousand years and continues to search.

docs.title: What they believe
docs.text:
• The mountain interior was worked before the Skyborn era. Caverns, tunnels, and structures predate any record the Keelwrights or the Archivists can confirm.
  • Somewhere in that deeper geography sit the shard-forges — the original places shards were made. The cult's name refers to a tradition of the anvil (the place of making) being something one searches for rather than works at.
  • The Keelwrights' rune-craft is a descendant of an older craft buried beneath their feet. The Keelwrights deny this.
  • Finding the shard-forges is a sacred duty, undertaken without expectation of completion.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Search. Active expeditions into Ankarum's mountain interior. Two to five per year, two to four searchers each.
  • Document. Every expedition's findings are recorded; the records are kept in a substitution cipher.
  • Train. Senior members teach deep-mountain travel craft to apprentices, one-to-one, over years.
  • Avoid attention. The cult operates beneath the threshold the Keelwright guild and the Inquisition would treat as worth disrupting. The cult's public profile is, by design, smaller than the cult actually is.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Hammer. A single senior figure, chosen by the cult by acclamation. Currently the dwarf Vellgar of the Dimmed Anvil — well over a hundred years old, lives publicly as a retired tunneller in a small Khalvar mining hamlet. Vellgar's role within the cult is not formally documented in any non-cult record; the Keelwrights are aware of him; the Inquisition is not.
  • Searchers. The cult's field operatives. Mostly old. About twenty active across Ankarum.
  • Sympathizers. A larger lay membership — neighbors, kin, and quiet supporters who host returning expeditions and pass small kindnesses to active members. The cult does not publish numbers; the Keelwrights and the Inquisition estimate ranges between "a few dozen" (the Inquisition's estimate) and "perhaps two hundred" (the senior Keelwrights' estimate; the Keelwrights have always tracked the cult more closely than they admit).

docs.title: Known figures
docs.text:
• Vellgar of the Dimmed Anvil. The senior. Publicly: a retired tunneller in his second century. Privately: the cult's senior figure for thirty-one years.
  • Belrun Stoneforger. A respected Khalvar Hold mason whose back rooms have, by quiet local knowledge, hosted Black Anvil expedition crews for two decades. The Inquisition has never questioned him. His sister Lannora Stonebeard is a senior Ember Faith Watch-Mother — the Stoneforger-Stonebeard family carries the Anvil-and-Faith dual tradition openly, the way many dwarven families in Khalvar do.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text:
Eccentric. Quietly tolerated. The cult has survived for a thousand years in part because it is too small to threaten the order and too persistent to discourage. Dwarven public opinion treats Black Anvil members as harmless seekers — slightly ridiculous, slightly admirable, certainly never dangerous. The mainstream dwarven philosophical view is that the search itself is the practice; finding anything would be incidental.

That the cult has never produced a shard in a thousand years of searching is, depending on whom one asks, either evidence of their fundamental mistake or evidence of the patience their tradition requires.

docs.title: Ember Faith
docs.text:
A dwarven splinter sect. Worship the lost Red Ember — the great shard that fell to Ankarum during the War of the Three Shards and vanished in an underground collapse before its bearer could be acclaimed. The Ember Faith waits for its return. The Ember Faith has been waiting for over two thousand years.

The faith is tolerated within Ankarum and across the five nations as a fringe variant of dwarven worship rather than full heresy. The Inquisition has not moved against them in living memory; the Order of the Pure Flame considers them harmless. Their chapter-houses operate openly in Khalvar Hold and a handful of mining communities. Their annual Vigil — held on the anniversary of the Ember's loss — is one of the oldest continuously-observed dwarven ceremonies.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Wait. The faith's central practice. The bearer will return. The Ember will be borne again. Time is not an obstacle.
  • Maintain the Vigils. Year-round Watching Lights at the chapter-houses; the great annual Vigil at the site of the original collapse (now a cave-in marked by a small shrine).
  • Keep the Records of Loss. Chronicles of dwarves who lost something significant — a child, a homestead, a craft, a name. The Records are the faith's social ledger; mourning is what the faith does for its people, and the Records remember.
  • Tend mourners. Dwarven funeral rites, year-after rites for the bereaved, the long quiet visit a Watch-Mother makes a year after a death.
  • Walk the Pilgrim Trail. To the cave-in site, once in every adult dwarven life by tradition.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Ember-Keeper. A single seat, by acclamation. Currently held by Ember-Keeper Rovenn Halrandsdotter, a dwarven cleric in her ninety-fourth year.
  • The Watch-Mothers. Senior lay women who tend the chapter-houses' Watching Lights. About fifteen across Ankarum.
  • The Bearers-in-Waiting. Initiated members; have completed the Pilgrim Trail and taken the Watcher's Oath. About sixty active.
  • The Mourners. Lay members. Several hundred across Ankarum; harder to count because membership is informal and often inherited.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Ember-Keeper Rovenn Halrandsdotter. Ninety-four years in body, seventy of those as a cleric of Melancholy and the Ember Faith both. Lives at the Khalvar chapter-house.
  • Watch-Mother Lannora Stonebeard. Senior Watch-Mother at the Khalvar Hold great chapter-house. Runs the largest annual Vigil.
  • Watch-Mother Berga of the Long Vault. Watches the cave-in shrine year-round; lives at the Pilgrim Trail's terminus.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Quietly respected. The Ember Faith is not the dwarven mainstream — most dwarves give the Twelve their formal devotion and the Ember a wistful nod — but the sect is treated as the dwarven nation's official keeper of grief and waiting. When a dwarven House loses something irrecoverable, it is the Ember Faith that holds the year-after rite.

docs.title: Keelwrights of Ankarum
docs.text: The dwarven engineering guild that, in their own slogan, literally holds Aetherion together. If the Gearwright Consortium keeps the continent moving, the Keelwrights keep it intact.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Rune-engineering. Inscribe and maintain the load-bearing runes that distribute the continent's structural stresses across its underlying skeleton.
  • Stabilizing Halls. Operate a dozen runic-engine chambers beneath Ankarum's mountains that absorb tectonic stress and release it as harmless heat. Crewed by specialist Wrights who live underground in months-long shifts.
  • Skyquake response. When the continent's structural integrity is challenged — a major impact, a deliberate sabotage, an unexplained tremor — the Keelwrights deploy. The senior skyquake-response Wright is one of the most dangerous and admired positions in the guild.
  • Mountain access. Control all licensed entry to Ankarum's mountain interior. Mining permits, excavation contracts, archaeological expeditions all pass through the guild.
  • Rune-craft licensing. Every legitimate rune-smith on the continent carries a Keelwright license. Unlicensed rune-work is prosecuted through the guild's own enforcement — penalty is de-licensing, a social and professional death.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The First Anvil — the guild's senior figure, a single seat, always a senior warrior-craftsman.
  • The Twelve Anvils — one per major guild specialty (load-bearing, stress-distribution, runic-engine maintenance, mountain-survey, skyquake-response, mining-licensure, rune-smithing standards, apprentice training, foreign liaison, legal, treasury, ritual). They form the guild council.
  • Keelwrights — about 500 full-licensed members, most based in Khalvar Hold, with smaller chapterhouses in Aurion, Cogbarrow, Moonbough, and Greenmere.
  • Journeymen — about 1,200. The working layer.
  • Apprentices — about 2,000. Most apprenticeships run twelve years.
  • The Stabilizers — about 80 Wrights specialized to crew the Stabilizing Halls. They take an oath: I will not abandon the Hall until relieved.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Keelwrights are respected almost universally. They are also feared by anyone who needs a license they can't get — unlicensed rune-craft is one of the few things every nation prosecutes the same way. Their guild discipline is famously strict. Their politics, famously quiet.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • travel-and-flight.md — how Aetherion moves; the Keelwrights' work is one half of why it stays whole.

docs.title: Aetheric Collegium
docs.text:
The continent's sanctioned scholarly body for the study of shards. Human and gnomish magisters, with Sylvari fellows referred in through the Council of Boughs, operating under direct Majestal Court oversight. Headquartered in Aurion at the Magisterium, a tower-and-vault complex in the city's Second District.

The Collegium is the official channel for everything related to shards: recovery research, provenance analysis, safe handling, vault construction, and the small body of theoretical work the Court permits to be published. Where the Lanternbearers practice shard-meditation outside any institution and the Order of the Pure Flame hunts unauthorized bearers, the Collegium is the calm institutional middle — the place where shards go after the Pure Flame catches their bearer.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Catalogue. Every shard the Order recovers (and every shard the Collegium independently acquires) is logged, measured, photographed, and assigned to a research wing.
  • Study. Each shard is subjected to a standard set of measurements — resonance, mass, light-passage, response to attempted bonding — then assigned to longer-term research based on what those measurements suggest.
  • Vault. The Magisterium's lower vaults hold the largest concentrated shard collection on the continent. Access is graduated; most fellows have admission to no more than three of the Magisterium's twelve research wings.
  • Publish. The Collegium produces a quarterly journal (the Aetheric Review) and an annual Symposium. Both are Court-sanctioned and predominantly safe.
  • Train. The Collegium runs a five-year initiate program; senior fellows mentor one or two initiates each. The Aetheric Collegium Initiate is a recognized credential carried by some of the continent's most respected scholars.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Magister Primus. A single seat. Held by Magister Primus Aldric Brightcrest (human, late seventies; twelve years in seat).
  • Senior Shard-Wardens. Seven, each running one of the major research divisions (Provenance, Phenomenology, Theory, Containment, Field Recovery, Records, Anchor Studies).
  • Senior Fellows. About twenty-five, leading specific research projects.
  • Fellows. About eighty, the body of credentialed researchers.
  • Initiates. About forty, in their five-year training cycle.
  • Visiting Scholars. Ten to fifteen across any given year, mostly Sylvari but a steady trickle from every nation.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Magister Primus Aldric Brightcrest. Twelve years in the seat; the most senior shard-scholar on the continent. Speaks rarely; when he does, the Court listens.
  • Senior Shard-Warden Lothair Marblescribe (Theory). The Collegium's most cited theoretical scholar; conservative.
  • Senior Shard-Warden Iolyth-wen-Vaelar Greylock (Anchor Studies). Has run the Grand Anchor research program for thirty-one years; quiet, exacting.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Collegium is the continent's intellectual establishment on shards — respected, well-funded, conservative. Its credentials carry weight in every nation. Its public output is measured and careful. Its silence on uncomfortable questions is taken, by most of the continent's scholarly community, as evidence that the questions are not yet ready to be answered. A few quieter colleagues read the silence differently.

docs.title: Lanternbearers
docs.text:
An outlawed mystical tradition of shard-practitioners scattered across the continent. They hold rituals in secret caves, treat shards as bearers of meaning the Court does not sanction, and pursue communion with relics through practices the Order of the Pure Flame burns them for.

They are real, persistent, and quieter than either the Court or the Order would like to admit.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Practice. Conduct shard rituals — meditation, invocation, sometimes deliberate bonding — that the Aetheric Collegium would call unsafe and the Order of the Pure Flame would call heretical. The practices are old.
  • Teach. Apprentice individuals into the work. The transmission is one-to-one, oral, slow. There are no Lanternbearer textbooks; the tradition lives in living teachers.
  • Pass relics. Move recovered shards between practitioners. Much smaller volume than the Aetheric Collegium's sanctioned distribution, and a much higher trust threshold.
  • Hide. Lanternbearer cells operate in remote caves, abandoned mines, mountain hermitages. Most cells number three to eight practitioners. A larger cell becomes a target.
  • Endure. Lanternbearers have been hunted for centuries. The tradition persists because it answers something the official cosmology does not.

docs.title: Structure (such as it is)
docs.text:
There is no central Lanternbearer organization. There are:

  • Cells. Three to eight practitioners per cell, geographically localized, usually in or near a known cave, hermitage, or remote mining tunnel.
  • Lanterns. Senior practitioners — fifteen years or more in the tradition. Each cell has one to three. The title comes from the practice of leading new initiates through their first ritual with a literal lantern, underground.
  • The Old Lanterns. A handful of practitioners sixty or more years in. They are known by reputation among other Lanterns but rarely seen.
  • Apprentices. Initiates in their first one to five years.

docs.title: Practices
docs.text:
• The First Lantern. A new initiate's first ritual. Held underground, by lantern-light, with one or two senior practitioners present. The initiate is shown a single shard and invited to listen.
  • The Long Listening. Daily meditation with a shard, alone, over years. Senior Lanternbearers have deep relationships with the relics they have been listening to, sometimes for decades.
  • The Ring. Group ritual. Five to nine practitioners sit around a single shard for a full night. Practiced rarely.
  • The Drift. A retreat into wilderness with no shard at all. Often follows a major ritual.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Order of the Pure Flame hunts Lanternbearers as unauthorized bearers. The Inquisition occasionally takes them as ordinary heretics. The Aetheric Collegium publicly disowns them. Within Aetherion's quieter scholarly circles, the Lanternbearers' practices are spoken of more carefully — they are old enough that some scholars will admit, in private, that the official position may be incomplete.

docs.title: Veil Folk
docs.text: A loose cross-nation network of scholarly heretics — readers, archivists, copyists, hedge-philosophers — who believe the official cosmology hides something the Majestal Court does not want known. They are not a single organization. They are a tendency: individuals who, separately, have started asking the same forbidden questions and have, slowly, found each other.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Read. Old texts, fragmentary records, unauthorized translations. The Veil Folk read what they are not supposed to read.
  • Compare notes. Through coded correspondence, anonymized pamphlets, and quiet meetings at libraries' margins. The network operates on small exchanges — a question here, an answer there. There is no central archive.
  • Write. Pamphlets, sealed letters, hidden marginalia. Most Veil Folk writing is destroyed before it circulates more than a handful of times. The few persistent texts are legend among them.
  • Hide. Most Veil Folk maintain cover lives — as legitimate scholars, archivists, clerks, midwives. The discipline of not being a Veil Folk visibly is, for most members, the daily work.
  • Convert no one. They do not proselytize. The risks of recruitment outweigh the benefits; the network grows by accident.

docs.title: Structure (such as it is)
docs.text:
There is no formal structure. There is no hierarchy. There is no membership roll. There are:

  • Knots. Small groups of three to seven who actually know each other personally. Most Veil Folk are in exactly one knot.
  • Ciphers. Shared codes for marginalia and correspondence. Each knot has its own cipher; some ciphers are mutually intelligible to allied knots.
  • The Hand. Persistent rumor of a coordinating figure — someone who connects knots when convenient. In practice the Hand is, in any given city, whichever member happens to be most connected at the time. The role is informal and rotates without ceremony.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The cipher exchange. Most knots use a substitution cipher keyed to a published commercial reference of some kind (a merchant's directory, a calendar of festivals, a list of tithes). Useless to anyone without the right edition.
  • The marginalia game. Veil Folk insert questions and observations in the margins of legitimate scholarly works. A later reader, knowing what to look for, can extract a conversation that spans decades.
  • The drop-shelf. A specific bookshelf in a specific shop where messages can be left under a known third-shelf book. Most cities have at least one.
  • The slow read. A Veil Folk scholar may take years to follow a single thread. The discipline of not being caught chasing a question is the discipline of patience.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Inquisition hunts them as ordinary heretics when they catch them. The Court treats them as a structural threat that must be tolerated because purging them outright would mean purging half the continent's senior scholars. They are not, in most ordinary Skyborn's lives, ever encountered. They are also, in most senior scholars' careers, met at least once.

docs.title: Gearwright's Consortium
docs.text: The gnomish engineering syndicate that runs propulsion, the Crowngear, drift mechanics, and most large-scale mechanical infrastructure on the continent. Headquartered in Cogbarrow's central works. If the Keelwrights keep Aetherion intact, the Consortium keeps it moving.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Drift control. Operate the Crowngear, the great mechanism that lets Aetherion be subtly steered against the prevailing winds.
  • Propulsion. Design and license the gnomish turbines, gravitic regulators, and stabilizers that move every airship, lift, and skybridge worth crossing.
  • Patents and licensing. Hold continent-wide monopolies on the high-end propulsion technology. Licenses to use Consortium designs are issued by guild authority and revoked at guild discretion.
  • Crowngear projects. Any sufficiently large mechanical project — anomaly-detection prototypes, military targeting arrays, the Tether-end lift mechanisms — passes through Consortium hands.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Senior Council. Twelve members. Set policy, allocate research funding, decide who gets licensed and who quietly doesn't. Werrik Steamspoke chairs.
  • Engineer-Wrights. Master engineers with full Consortium credentials. About 40 across the continent.
  • Journeyman engineers. About 200 continent-wide. The working layer.
  • Apprentices. About 600. Mostly gnomish; some half-elven or human.
  • The Patent Office. Administrative arm. Where licenses are signed, contested, revoked. Famously slow.
  • The Calibration Shop. Operational arm. Where the Crowngear is actually adjusted, day to day. Lives three levels underground.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Werrik Steamspoke (Chair of the Senior Council). The Consortium's most ambitious and accomplished leader. Multiple major mechanism-systems built in one working lifetime; widely admired among the gnomish craft community.
  • Engineer-Wright Theryssa Twogear. Drift-control specialist. The technical brain behind the Crowngear's daily operation. Apolitical by reputation.
  • Engineer-Wright Pollix Glasswork. Optics and resonance. Senior, mentor to a generation of journeymen.
  • Patent Clerk Brakk Pendulum. Runs the Patent Office archives. Bureaucratic and immovable in equal measure.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The licensing letter. Every Consortium product carries a small brass plate stamped with the patent number, the licensed user's name, and the expiration date. Operating without one is grounds for confiscation, and the confiscation is delegated to the Skyward Inquisition when the gear touches anything regulated.
  • The Calibration Reports. Quarterly. Published in narrow technical journals. Senior council, senior nobles, and the Inquisition read them; almost nobody else can parse them.
  • The Convention. Annual gathering of all Engineer-Wrights and senior journeymen in Cogbarrow. Three days.
  • Quiet enforcement. When a competitor crosses a line, the response is technical — a turbine that fails three weeks later, a regulator that produces incorrect data. Plausibly an accident; demonstrably the Consortium's hand to another engineer.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Universally feared by independent inventors and universally relied on by everyone else. The Consortium's grip on propulsion is one of the most absolute monopolies in the five nations, and the Court has no interest in breaking it — without the Consortium, Aetherion drifts where the winds put it.

docs.title: Bank of Aurion
docs.text:
The continent's largest financial institution. Headquartered in Aurion's First District, with chartered branch offices in Khalvar Hold, Cogbarrow, Moonbough, and Greenmere. Holds noble debts, funds wars, underwrites continent-spanning trade, and issues the "faith coin" — the standardized Court-approved currency most Skyborn carry.

The Bank's vault is famously deep, famously well-guarded, and famously not open to inspection by anyone outside the Bank's own Sealed Counters.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Lend. Noble Houses with long-running campaigns, ambitious estates, or expensive marriages borrow from the Bank. The terms are tight.
  • Hold. Deposits from nobles, guilds, and a handful of senior commoners. The Bank's discretion is its main product.
  • Mint. The faith coin — standardized weight, sealed denomination, recognized in every nation. Issued under Court authority.
  • Underwrite. Trade fleets, salvage expeditions, large guild ventures. The Bank's underwriting carries enough weight that an unwritten venture is widely considered unfundable.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Respected, feared, and quietly resented. The Bank's senior officers attend the Court's major functions; the Bank's contributions to the Court's calendar are conspicuous. Persistent rumor holds that the Bank's vault contains more than coin — that something of unusual value backs the faith coin's stability. The Bank has never confirmed and has never denied.

docs.title: Cloud Knights of Aurion
docs.text:
Highcrown's elite knightly order. Sworn personally to Majesty and the High Throne, operating the continent's finest airships — the great Cloud Lances, the kind of vessels ordinary Skyborn glimpse from the ground once or twice in a lifetime. About a hundred knights, supported by a hundred and fifty crew. Smaller than the Skyguard's airship corps and far more selective.

The Order is the Court's most visible ceremonial force. Cloud Knights ride at every major Aurion festival, escort the Throne to its public appearances, and are dispatched on diplomatic and high-prestige operational missions. They are, by long tradition, the army the Court sends when the army matters less than the statement of sending an army.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Operate the Cloud Lances. Six great line-airships in active service, each crewed by a captain, two lieutenants, and about twenty Cloud Knights and crew. The vessels are the continent's largest civilian-visible craft and the Order's primary instrument.
  • Escort the Throne. Ceremonial honor guard at the Majestal Palace. Two-knight rotation at major doors; full escort during the Throne's public appearances.
  • Project force. When the Court wants to be seen to be acting, the Cloud Knights are dispatched. Their arrival in a regional capital tends to resolve the underlying political problem before any actual force is required.
  • Train. A long initiate program — five years minimum, often seven. The Order's recruits are nearly all from senior noble Houses; the exceptions are talented Skyguard captains who transfer in by sponsored examination.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Grand Captain. Apex. Single seat. Currently Grand Captain Veron of House Solnaris (human, mid-fifties; eight years in seat).
  • Lieutenant-Captains. Six, one per Cloud Lance. The senior field officers.
  • Knights. About a hundred sworn.
  • Initiates. Twenty to thirty at any time in the five-year program.
  • Crew. About a hundred and fifty supporting personnel — airshipwrights, riggers, gunners, surgeons, cooks. Most are not Knights and never will be; the crew positions are their own respected craft tradition.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Grand Captain Veron of House Solnaris. The Order's senior officer; House Solnaris is the engineered hereditary line that produces Muse's vessels (Maestra Cylleth's House), and Veron is Cylleth's cousin by lineage. Eight years in the seat.
  • Lieutenant-Captain Aldra of House Goldensword. Commands the Cloud Lance Indomitable, the Order's flagship.
  • Lieutenant-Captain Wendric Highmarch. Commands the Cloud Lance Resolute; the Order's most-decorated field officer.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Glorious, somewhat insufferable, universally photographed. The Cloud Knights are the continent's romantic military image — the army that exists to be admired. Their actual operational record is solid; their political record is uniformly loyal; their public visibility is enormous. Children who grow up in Aurion's Lower Quarter watching the Cloud Lances pass overhead frequently dream of joining the Order; almost none of them do.

docs.title: Crown Assembly
docs.text:
Highcrown's noble class organized as a deliberative body. Fifty-three Houses hold Assembly seats; twenty-three of those Houses also hold the more powerful Quorum of Sanctified Houses inside the Majestal Court itself.

The Assembly votes on land disputes, trade tariffs, succession rulings, and inter-House precedence. It does not vote on doctrine — that is the Court's reserve — but it shapes nearly everything else about how Aurion is governed below the level of the High Throne.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Adjudicate. Inter-noble disputes that don't rise to Court attention.
  • Tax. Approve continent-wide trade tariffs and Aurion-specific levies.
  • Confirm. Marriages, inheritances, and adoptions among the seated Houses.
  • Lobby. The Assembly's twenty-three Quorum Houses each hold a Court vote. The other thirty Houses spend their political capital influencing the Quorum.
  • Spend. The Assembly's collective expenditures — festivals, public works, the Assembly Hall's own staffing — represent a meaningful share of Aurion's annual budget.

docs.title: How it actually works
docs.text:
Assembly politics are alliances, marriages, and quiet exchanges of favor. Public votes are usually formalities; the real decisions are made in private over the course of weeks, often months. The Bank of Aurion is rumored to lend more freely to Houses with promising voting alignments. Bribery is technically a hangable offense; in practice it is a constant low-grade pressure that no one quite calls bribery.

Most Houses own at least one shard, displayed publicly or held in family vaults. Shard ownership is a marker of seniority and Court favor as much as wealth.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Assembly is the Court's quiet workhorse. Ordinary Skyborn rarely see it operate; they feel it through the prices in the market and the laws that touch their guilds. Its members are widely known by name and rank but rarely known personally outside their own districts.

docs.title: Majestal Court
docs.text: The theocratic ruling body of Aetherion. Interprets the will of the Twelve, sets doctrine, and controls orthodoxy through sermons, festivals, the calendar, censorship, and the Skyward Inquisition as its enforcement arm. Seated in the Majestal Palace at the heart of Aurion. The most powerful single institution on the continent.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Doctrine. Issue official theological pronouncements. Approve festivals. Authorize new sanctuaries. Determine which sects are tolerated, heretical, or treasonous.
  • Calendar. Manage the liturgical year. Major festivals (Maelstrom's Storm-Welcome, Mirth's Midwinter, Mortis's Long Quiet, Memory's Counting-Days) are Court productions.
  • Adjudication. Hear appeals from the Inquisition. Render judgment on inter-noble disputes that touch theology. Confirm or void the noble Houses' marriages, inheritances, and legitimacy claims.
  • Resource control. Authorize Skyguard deployments above the divisional level. Sign continent-wide trade decrees. Tax the noble Houses through tithe-equivalent obligations.
  • Public face. Maintain the visible presence of the Twelve through scripted appearances — Majesty's incarnate body at court ceremonies, Mercy's annual hospice blessings, Mortis's grave-counting at year's end.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The High Throne. A single seat occupied by Majesty. Currently held by Hadrian of House Auremont. The Throne speaks for the pantheon when the pantheon speaks publicly.
  • The Council of Twelve Voices. Twelve seats, one per M. By doctrine, each Voice is the god themself. In practice only four of the Twelve reside in Aurion and attend in person — Majesty, Mercy, Malice, and Muse. The other eight reside elsewhere on the continent, and for each the Court has appointed a Senior Speaker to hold the Voice's Council seat as proxy. The seat remains the god's; routine attendance is the Speaker's. The Voice of Madness has been vacant for six years — the prior Speaker died and the goddess has not chosen a successor.
  • The Quorum of Sanctified Houses. Twenty-three noble Houses with hereditary seats. Vote on land, trade, and noble-precedence matters.
  • The Clerks of Aurelium. Career bureaucrats. Run the Court's day-to-day operations. Not titled, not voting, indispensable.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Hadrian Auremont (Voice of Majesty). The High Throne. Public face of the pantheon. Speaks at all major occasions.
  • Senior Speaker Theron Vethraine (Speaker for Memory). Holds Memory's Council seat in her absence — Memory's incarnation resides in Moonbough. Senior archivist and informal historian; has held the seat for forty-one years. Speaks slowly. Knows where every body is buried, often literally.
  • High Chamberlain Brienna of House Caltherine. Runs the Palace's operational staff — schedules, security, ceremonial logistics. Reports directly to the Throne.
  • Senior Speaker Wulfrik Brynhalla (Speaker for Might). Holds Might's Council seat in his absence — Might's incarnation resides in Khalvar Hold. Former Khalvar warlord, promoted to the Council eleven years ago.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The Sealed Address. When the Court announces something significant, it is read simultaneously in all five capitals at sunset. Mandatory attendance for sanctioned clergy.
  • The Pilgrimage Calendar. Determines which sites are blessed, when, by which Voice. Cities live and die by it.
  • The Censor's Wax. Books, pamphlets, and printed materials touching the Twelve, the Pit, or the Skyfall require Court-issued wax-seals to circulate legally.
  • The Quiet Conversation. Senior Court members do not threaten in public. They invite the noble in question to tea. Tea is a ranked invitation: morning tea is informational; afternoon tea is corrective; evening tea is final.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Universally respected, deeply feared in specific ways, and almost never disobeyed in public. The Court's grip on Aetherion is doctrinal, ceremonial, and procedural at once. Most Skyborn defer without thinking about it; the rare ones who don't usually have a tea invitation in their future.

docs.title: Order of the Pure Flame
docs.text: The Court's militant arm for shard-related crimes. Where the Skyward Inquisition handles heresy, Ground-sympathy, and doctrinal threats, the Pure Flame handles shard-bearers — those who possess, use, or transport relics without sanction. They guard the Court-authorized vaults, lead shard-recovery crusades, and pursue unauthorized bearers as fugitives across the five nations.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Guard. Authorized shard vaults across the five nations are protected by Pure Flame detachments — typically a senior officer plus four to eight sworn brothers and sisters. The largest detachment is at the Order's Aurion chapterhouse.
  • Hunt. Unauthorized shard-bearers — anyone holding, wearing, or wielding a shard without an Aetheric Collegium license — are crusader targets. The Order pursues them across the continent, with sealed Court warrants giving them jurisdiction in every nation.
  • Recover. Lost relics — particularly the named historical shards — are the Order's ideological mission. Generations of recruits join with the hope of being the one who recovers a master shard.
  • Burn. A Pure Flame crusader who fails their hunt, defects, or is found to have hidden a recovered shard for personal use is burned — ceremonially put to death by their own brothers. The ritual is old. It still happens.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Flame-Marshal. Apex commander. A single seat. Currently held by Marshal Aldric Veteraine of House Veteraine — a cadet branch of House Auremont, distantly related to the High Throne.
  • The Sworn Captains. Eight in total — six regional commanders (Aurion, Khalvar Hold, Cogbarrow, Moonbough, Greenmere, Tether's End) plus two roving Crusade Captains who travel with active hunts.
  • Sworn Brothers and Sisters. About 400 active across the continent. Full members of the Order, oath-bound, armored, named.
  • Postulants. About 150. The pipeline. Five-year postulancy; many wash out.
  • The Burning Chamber. Not a rank — a place. The vaulted room beneath the Aurion chapterhouse where ceremonial executions happen. Existence is widely known; access is restricted to Sworn members.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Flame-Marshal Aldric Veteraine. Sixteen years in the seat. Speaks formally. Believes the Order's mission correctly.
  • Sworn Captain Brenna Stormbrand. Roving Crusade Captain, currently active in eastern Ankarum and the Khalvar trade routes. The Order's best hunter; eleven recoveries in nine years.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The sworn warrant. Pure Flame warrants are open-jurisdiction. They overrule local law and Inquisition warrants both. A Pure Flame Captain can compel a Skyguard division to assist if necessary.
  • The flame-iron. Every Sworn brother and sister carries a small, blessed brand-iron that resonates in the presence of an active shard. Imprecise but reliable in the gross sense.
  • The hunt-banner. When the Order declares an active hunt, a banner is unfurled at the chapterhouse. Public; visible across the city. The banner stays until the hunt resolves.
  • The Greater Burning. The Order's central rite. Held twice a year in the Burning Chamber. Outsiders are not present.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Devout, dangerous, and respected. The Order is small enough that most Skyborn never meet a Sworn brother; large enough that everyone knows what the flame-sigil on the shoulder means. Their hunts make news. Their executions do not.

docs.title: Skyguard
docs.text: Highcrown's standing army. The continent's largest organized military force — about twelve thousand active personnel under arms — operating airships, conventional infantry, cavalry, siege engineers, and the largest single garrison structure on Aetherion. Reports nominally to the High Throne through the Voice of Might's seat on the Council of Twelve Voices.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Garrison. Standing garrisons in Aurion (the largest), Khalvar Hold, Moonbough, Cogbarrow, Greenmere, and Tether's End.
  • Patrol. Inter-nation borders, the major trade routes, the Anchor's upper approaches.
  • Operate airships. About thirty airships of various classes, docked principally at Aurion's Skyhold and Khalvar Hold's Aerie.
  • Train. Continuous training cycles across the major garrisons. Specialist schools by location (airship crews at Khalvar, cavalry at Moonbough, artillery at Cogbarrow, medical at Greenmere).

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Lord Marshal. Apex; single seat. Currently Lord Marshal Caspian Brightcrest (human, late fifties; nine years in seat).
  • The Marshalry. Six Marshals beneath the Lord Marshal, each commanding a division: Aurion Field, Khalvar Field, Northern Field, Western Field, Southern Field, and the Aerial Marshal (airship corps).
  • Colonel-Generals. Twelve regiment commanders.
  • Captains. About 80 company commanders.
  • Rankers. The rank-and-file, about ten thousand strong.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Lord Marshal Caspian Brightcrest. Brother to the Aetheric Collegium's Magister Primus; the Brightcrest line produces scholars and generals in alternating generations.
  • Aerial Marshal Iverra Stormbrand. Commands the airship corps; cousin to Brenna Stormbrand of the Pure Flame and Senior Speaker Bertran Stormbrand at the Court.
  • Marshal Korunna Halrandsdotter. Commands the Khalvar Field garrison; coordinates with the Keelwrights on Ankarum's joint operations.
  • Captain Sevran Truefont. Commands Aurion's First Company; ceremonial duties at most Court functions.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Respected, professional, slightly slow to mobilize. The Skyguard's training is famously thorough and slightly old-fashioned. The patrol rotation means most communities see Skyguard personnel regularly but rarely the same faces. The institution carries the prestige of being the army that protects the continent; the bureaucracy of being the largest peacetime force on Aetherion has worn a certain amount of dashing off its actual culture.

docs.title: Skyward Inquisition
docs.text: The Majestal Court's enforcement arm. Hunts heresy, Ground-sympathy, and forbidden shard lore through informants, closed trials, and quiet disappearances. Feared, respected, and — by most ordinary Skyborn — considered necessary.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Investigate. Maintain a continent-wide network of paid informants, sealed case files, and standing surveillance details on registered persons of interest.
  • Detain. Take suspects into custody on Inquisition warrants. Hold them in the cells beneath the Aurion headquarters or in district lockups across the five nations.
  • Try. Conduct closed trials before three-judge panels of senior Inquisitors. The accused are heard; defenders are limited; verdicts are not appealed except to the Majestal Court itself, which has reversed seven cases in the last thirty years.
  • Punish. Sentences range from indentured service to permanent exile to execution. Public executions are rare; most sentences are carried out quietly.
  • Confiscate. Recover unauthorized shards and turn them over to the Aetheric Collegium or the Order of the Pure Flame, depending on classification.

docs.title: Ranks
docs.text:
• Inquisitor — field operative, recent oath, squad member.
  • Inquisitor-Captain — squad leader, five to ten years' service. Authorizes raids.
  • Inquisitor-Warden — specialized roles (vault chief, archive, evidence). Rarely in the field.
  • Inquisitor-Commander — senior field administrator. Authorizes major operations.
  • Senior Inquisitor — the apex public role. Speaks for the Court on doctrine.

Each nation has a regional Inquisition administration; all five report up to Aurion. Aurion HQ runs continent-wide priorities, sets doctrine, and operates the master case-file archive.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Senior Inquisitor Cassia Velaspar. The apex. Decades of exemplary service; the public face of the Inquisition's "stern but fair" doctrine.
  • Inquisitor-Commander Veska Orval. Cassia's deputy. Runs daily operations at Aurion HQ. Skyguard veteran.
  • Inquisitor-Warden Tomas Kestrin. Aurion vault chief. Bookish, scrupulous, treats artifacts with more care than people.
  • Inquisitor-Captain Sevran Marrek. District officer for the Low Quarters and mid-quarter periphery of Aurion. A career professional, present at most public Inquisition actions in those districts.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The notebook. Captains and above keep personal case-files in clothbound notebooks. Lost notebooks trigger internal review.
  • The whistle. Standard issue. Heard for hundreds of feet through walls. An Inquisitor in trouble gets reinforcements very fast.
  • The bell-rope. Larger Inquisition installations have concealed bell-ropes that summon the district patrol.
  • Sealed warrants. Warrant-bearer Inquisitors have priority over local law and can compel cooperation from Skyguard, Crown Assembly, and most nobles. Only the Court can override a warrant; few are willing to try.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Most Skyborn never deal with the Inquisition directly, and don't want to. The Inquisition's public position is procedural and unsentimental: enforce the Inquisition Policy, close cases cleanly, document everything. The institution's grip on Aurion society is heavy enough that fear of attention is a real social force, not merely a moral one.

docs.title: Archivists of Moonbough
docs.text: The Sylvari scholarly order that maintains the Great Library of Moonbough — the largest accumulated archive on the continent. The Library's stacks descend through the canopy city's roots into chambers older than the Skyborn era.
